title: Warnings and diagnostics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Warnings and diagnostics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Warnings and diagnostics
sources.claim: A diagnostic identifies a violated syntax or constraint or a tool-detected risk; its phase, primary location, severity, and first causal message guide repair.

sources.locator: SEI CERT C Coding Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Warnings and diagnostics

canonicalExplanation.summary: A diagnostic identifies a violated syntax or constraint or a tool-detected risk; its phase, primary location, severity, and first causal message guide repair.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A missing semicolon causes a parser diagnostic near the following token, so the learner inspects the preceding statement before changing unrelated code. Your goal is to repair syntax and constraint violations reported by a strict compiler invocation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Preprocess, compile, assemble and link. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A diagnostic identifies a violated syntax or constraint or a tool-detected risk; its phase, primary location, severity, and first causal message guide repair. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Warnings are tool-configurable, one cause can cascade into many messages, and no diagnostic is required for every undefined or incorrect behavior. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A program with no compiler warnings is guaranteed to be defined, correct, and portable.

includes: Use diagnostic locations and warning categories instead of ignoring warnings.
Repair syntax and constraint violations reported by a strict compiler invocation.
excludes: Warnings are tool-configurable, one cause can cascade into many messages, and no diagnostic is required for every undefined or incorrect behavior.

outcomes: Repair syntax and constraint violations reported by a strict compiler invocation.

examples.title: Warnings and diagnostics: defined case
examples.situation: A missing semicolon causes a parser diagnostic near the following token, so the learner inspects the preceding statement before changing unrelated code.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A missing semicolon causes a parser diagnostic near the following token, so the learner inspects the preceding statement before changing unrelated code.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Treat clean diagnostics as bounded tool evidence, not proof; compile with strict warnings and still test behavior, contracts, portability, and undefined-behavior risks.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Treat clean diagnostics as bounded tool evidence, not proof; compile with strict warnings and still test behavior, contracts, portability, and undefined-behavior risks.

examples.title: Warnings and diagnostics: boundary case
examples.situation: Strict warnings report an implicit narrowing or suspicious comparison even though object code could be emitted, prompting an explicit correctness review.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Strict warnings report an implicit narrowing or suspicious comparison even though object code could be emitted, prompting an explicit correctness review.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Warnings are tool-configurable, one cause can cascade into many messages, and no diagnostic is required for every undefined or incorrect behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A program with no compiler warnings is guaranteed to be defined, correct, and portable.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair syntax and constraint violations reported by a strict compiler invocation.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A program with no compiler warnings is guaranteed to be defined, correct, and portable.
misconceptions.correction: Treat clean diagnostics as bounded tool evidence, not proof; compile with strict warnings and still test behavior, contracts, portability, and undefined-behavior risks.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: A program with no compiler warnings is guaranteed to be defined, correct, and portable.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Warnings and diagnostics: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Warnings are tool-configurable, one cause can cascade into many messages, and no diagnostic is required for every undefined or incorrect behavior.

recap.summary: You should now be able to repair syntax and constraint violations reported by a strict compiler invocation. Keep this boundary in view: Warnings are tool-configurable, one cause can cascade into many messages, and no diagnostic is required for every undefined or incorrect behavior.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Warnings and diagnostics using a concrete C rule rather than the optional analogy.
Why is this claim incorrect: A program with no compiler warnings is guaranteed to be defined, correct, and portable.
Apply the checkpoint 'strict warnings' to the boundary case from memory.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Library headers and declarations”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
